title: Relations, Rows, Domains, Keys, and Missing Information · Relational Lens: Super Candidate Primary Keys
summary: Support tickets, teams, members, and events must become a precise relational model without duplicate identity or invented missing values. Planned artifact: Add and verify the sql super candidate primary keys increment in a key-and-null-aware relational model. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish superkeys, candidate keys, primary keys, alternate keys, and stable surrogate identifiers.
Explain relations, tuples, attributes, domains, degree, cardinality, and why row order is not relational meaning.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: Model teams, members, tickets, and events where members may be unassigned, ticket titles repeat, and one ticket has 2 events. Declare row grain, candidate keys, cardinalities, NULL meaning, dependencies, and lossless relationships. Competency probe: Distinguish superkeys, candidate keys, primary keys, alternate keys, and stable surrogate identifiers. A defensible solution applies Super Candidate Primary Keys, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to a key-and-null-aware relational model.

content.code:
-- Worked focus: sql-super-candidate-primary-keys — Distinguish superkeys, candidate keys, primary keys, alternate keys, and stable surrogate identifiers.
SELECT t.ticket_id, t.owner_id, m.name AS owner_name
FROM tickets AS t
LEFT JOIN members AS m ON m.member_id = t.owner_id
WHERE t.owner_id IS NULL OR m.active = 1
ORDER BY t.ticket_id;
content.caption: Runnable relational query or data-system contract example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.
